serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of pasta ai funghi e asparagi is approximately 350 g, comprising a full plate of cooked pasta topped with sautéed mushrooms, asparagus, and cream sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with cream sauce and vegetables — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Pasta cooked (FDC ID 20420), Heavy cream (FDC ID 01053), Mushrooms raw (FDC ID 11260), Asparagus cooked (FDC ID 11012), Butter (FDC ID 01001), Parmesan cheese (FDC ID 01032)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for pasta ai funghi e asparagi as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked pasta, heavy cream, mushrooms, asparagus, butter, and Parmesan cheese, weighted by a representative recipe proportion (approximately 55% pasta, 20% cream sauce, 15% mushrooms, 10% asparagus). Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for a cream-based pasta dish of this type.

gi_source.title: Pasta, cooked — GI values from the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 (Atkinson et al.) and University of Sydney GI Database; cream sauce and vegetable additions lower effective GI relative to plain pasta
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cooked pasta (spaghetti/fettuccine) has a documented GI of approximately 40–50 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). The addition of cream sauce, mushrooms, and asparagus does not substantially raise the GI; fat and protein in the cream sauce further blunt glycemic response. A GI of 45 is used as a conservative mid-range estimate for this cream-based pasta dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 71.75) / 100 ≈ 32.3, rounded to 32.0, based on 350 g serving with 20.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 735 mg sodium, representing about 32% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertension management; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load and refined carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 32 per serving and 71.75 g total carbohydrate per 350 g plate, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a serving and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to moderate postprandial glucose response; the cream and fat content partially blunts glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from heavy cream and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 350 g serving delivers approximately 18.9 g saturated fat, exceeding the AHA recommended daily limit of 13 g for a 2,000 kcal diet; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose this dish only occasionally and consider requesting a lighter cream preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pasta ai Funghi e Asparagi is a classic Italian pasta dish featuring cooked pasta — typically fettuccine, tagliatelle, or penne — tossed with sautéed mushrooms and asparagus in a rich heavy cream sauce, often finished with Parmesan cheese, butter, garlic, and black pepper. The dish originates from the Italian culinary tradition of pairing earthy forest mushrooms (commonly porcini, cremini, or button mushrooms) with the delicate bitterness of asparagus in a velvety cream reduction. The texture is silky and coating, with the pasta absorbing the cream sauce, while the mushrooms contribute an umami depth and the asparagus provides a slight crunch and grassy note. At Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore in Singapore, this dish is served as a restaurant-quality main course priced at approximately SGD 29.32. Nutritionally, a 350 g serving provides approximately 648 kcal, 20.3 g protein, 32.2 g fat (of which 18.9 g is saturated), and 71.75 g carbohydrate. The dish has a low glycemic index (estimated GI 45) due to the pasta matrix and fat content of the cream sauce, but carries a moderate glycemic load per serving. It is vegetarian but not vegan or dairy-free, and contains gluten and dairy allergens. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension (sodium ~735 mg per serving), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate GL, large carbohydrate load), and cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat). It is best consumed in moderated portions by individuals managing these conditions.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite Italian pasta dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked pasta, heavy cream, mushrooms, asparagus, butter, and Parmesan cheese using representative recipe proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous pasta entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (cooked pasta FDC 20420, heavy cream FDC 01053, mushrooms FDC 11260, asparagus cooked FDC 11012, butter FDC 01001, Parmesan FDC 01032) weighted by representative recipe proportions. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are not directly reported by a single source for this composite dish; these values carry higher uncertainty. No sentinel -1 values are used in this record as all fields have been estimated from component data. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on cooked pasta GI range (40–50) from the University of Sydney GI Database; cream and fat content further moderate glycemic response. This dish is served at Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey data source). The food_group is retained as Grains and staples consistent with the input record, as pasta is the primary energy-contributing ingredient. Italian cuisine does not require mandatory non-English local-language names under Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules; local_names fields are null accordingly.
